summary: autotel-devtools 6.1.2: minified bundle keyword matches require human review
details: The package ships minified/bundled CLI and server entry points (dist/cli.cjs, dist/cli.js, dist/index.cjs, dist/index.js, dist/server/index.cjs, dist/server/index.js, dist/widget.global.js) where keyword co-occurrence patterns matched on Buffer.from(..., 'base64') decoding plus references to 'curl', 'ping', and 'POST' inside the same files. These are pattern-level signals only; in minified single-line bundles such tokens routinely co-locate even for benign devtool functionality (e.g., a ping/health endpoint, base64-encoded asset embedding, a CLI that shells out to curl as a documented option). Without a traced reachable code path that demonstrates installer-side harm — for example, an install lifecycle hook, a top-level require side effect that exfiltrates env vars or credentials, or a hardcoded attacker-controlled destination tied to a network sink — these matches are not sufficient to classify as an attack. No package.json lifecycle script evidence is present in the findings to indicate auto-execution on install. Recommend human review of the bundled sources to confirm intent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
